coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposites composed of poly (N-vinyl carbazole) functionalized carbon dots and their redox properties: Towards promising opto-electro transfer devices
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Yifeng
Li, Chenghao
Wang, Yuping
Liu, Cailin
Ren, Xianyan
coredata.dc:description: Poly (N-vinyl carbazole) functionalized carbon dots (PVK-CDots) exhibiting significant photoinduced redox properties show great potential to be applied in opto-electro devices and systems. Here, we further make sure the PVK-CDots are promising candidates for developing solar cells after we designed a “one-pot” procedure to prepare PVK-CDots nanocomposites with high carbon content. The “one-pot” procedure is such that when citric acid is thermally carbonized into a carbon core, the N-vinyl carbazole is thermally initiated in situ to polymerize on the surface of the carbon core as passivation layer. The prepared PVK-CDots containing very high carbon content (about 36 %) are strongly absorptive and therefore effective in photon harvesting and also brightly fluorescent across the visible spectral region, which as expected exhibits significant photoinduced redox properties. Taking perovskite solar cell as an example, the PVK-CDots are qualified to act as a hole transport layer (HTL) material according to the alignment of energy levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanocomposites
Poly (N-vinyl carbazole)
Solar cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The application in Perovskite solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An illustration on the structure of PVK-CDots, specifically the carbon dots surface binding and passivation by the carbazole moieties.

1H NMR of PVK-CDots-C (A), commercial PVK (B), PVK-CDots (C), and control sample prepared by treating the blank NVC under the same conditions but without CA (D). 1H NMR (500 MHz, chloroform-d) δ 8.1 ∼ 5.7 (H2 ∼ H8), 5.4 ∼ 4.4 (H1), 3.7 ∼ 2.2 (H9), 2.2 ∼ 0.6 (H10). The crossed peaks were related to chloroform-d in the NMR solvent, and the circled sharp peaks were on account of remaining methanol and hexanes from the purification of the dot sample for NMR test.

Absorption (ABS) spectra of the PVK-CDots (——), PVK-CDots-C (------), and control sample extracted from blank NVC (……) in o-dichlorobenzene solvent. Insert: photos for the o-dichlorobenzene solution of PVK-CDots (A), the CA-produced CDots in o-dichlorobenzene (B), the o-dichlorobenzene solution of the control sample (C), and the water solution of CA-produced CDots (D).
The normalized fluorescence spectra of the PVK-CDots excited at 400–600 nm in 20 nm increments. (○: the corresponding fluorescence quantum yields).

Stem-Volmer quenching plots for fluorescence quantum yields of the PVK-CDots in o-dichlorobenzene with 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) (——) and N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) (……) used as the electron-accepting and electron-donating quenchers, respectively, in organic solvent.
Schematic illustration of the energy levels for the PVK-CDots perovskite solar cell system.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposites composed of poly (N-vinyl carbazole) functionalized carbon dots and their redox properties: Towards promising opto-electro transfer devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly (N-vinyl carbazole) functionalized carbon dots (PVK-CDots) exhibiting significant photoinduced redox properties show great potential to be applied in opto-electro devices and systems. Here, we further make sure the PVK-CDots are promising candidates for developing solar cells after we designed a “one-pot” procedure to prepare PVK-CDots nanocomposites with high carbon content. The “one-pot” procedure is such that when citric acid is thermally carbonized into a carbon core, the N-vinyl carbazole is thermally initiated in situ to polymerize on the surface of the carbon core as passivation layer. The prepared PVK-CDots containing very high carbon content (about 36 %) are strongly absorptive and therefore effective in photon harvesting and also brightly fluorescent across the visible spectral region, which as expected exhibits significant photoinduced redox properties. Taking perovskite solar cell as an example, the PVK-CDots are qualified to act as a hole transport layer (HTL) material according to the alignment of energy levels.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the family of well-known polymeric photoconductive materials is poly (N-vinyl carbazole) (PVK), which has extensively used in photovoltaic devices and many related fields by virtue of its good hole transporting properties. Generally, PVK has been widely reported and studied as a host matrix blended with charge-transport species as fillers in nanocomposites [1–7]. The filler is crucial to the potential performance of nanocomposites, to which much attention has been paid. Among the existed ones, of particular interest and significance are nano sized carbon allotropes, from fullerenes at the zero-dimension, carbon nanotubes at the one-dimension [8] to more recently graphene at the two-dimension [9].

Nevertheless, there is a tremendous challenge of the homogeneous dispersity of the nanoscale carbon allotropes in the PVK matrix, resulting in the relatively low degree of functionalization, and only low weight/weight (w/w) or weight/volume (w/v) concentrations of the carbon allotropes. For example, a PVK-C60 thin film was reported, in which the C60 concentration is lower than 4 wt % [10]; most of PVK-CNTs and PVK-graphene composites exhibit about 5 ∼ 6 mass % incorporation of carbon [11,12]. Low content of the carbon allotropes will lead to an inadequate number of acceptors to hold the charges, and poor dispersion will incur charge tunneling through the carbon species.

Carbon dots (CDots), which are characteristic of special “core-shell” microstructure as well as unique electronic and photophysical properties, are considered as ideal candidates to photovoltaic devices and many related fields when functionalized by electronic polymers ̶ e.g. PVK. CDots are generally identified as small carbon nanoparticles (below 10 nm) with surface passivation via a soft corona-like layer of most organic molecules [13]. Structurally and mechanically agrees with the concept of CDots that polymers can play the role of passivation layer [14,15]. Therefore, the preparation of CDots is to obtain CDots-polymer nanocomposites, in which the CDots are uniformly dispersed in the polymer matrix as the filler and tightly interacted with the matrix. More importantly, the absorption, fluorescence emission and intriguing photoinduced redox properties of CDots are beneficial to improve the energy conversion efficiency of devices [16,17].

For example, the advantages of CDots versus fullerenes in functionalization by PVK have been reported by our team [18]. The PVK–CDots were found to possess more absorptive characteristics and therefore more effective in photon harvesting throughout the visible spectral band, which not only show significant photoinduced electron accepting characteristics but also extraordinarily strong electron donating capabilities that are not obtained from PVK–fullerenes. The carbon content in the PVK–CDots is 8 % ∼ 10 %, higher than that of reported other carbon allotropes in electronic polymers.

To further increase the carbon content in the PVK-CDots, we designed another preparation procedure. Briefly, the PVK-CDots nanocomposites were prepared via one-pot thermal-induced carbonization of citric acid as the carbon core and thermal-initiated in situ polymerization of N-vinyl carbazole as the surface passivation layer. The citric acid has been well accepted as carbon source to prepare high -performance CDots [19,20]. By using carbon source instead of carbon nanomaterials to mix with monomers, problems such as weak dispersibility of carbon materials and their uneven dispersion in the matrix are avoided, and the carbon content in the composites can be greatly improved.

There is no doubt that the well dispersed and high content of nanoscale carbon allotropes in the matrix is beneficial to enhance the photoelectric properties of composite materials. The redox properties and the related potential performances as promising opto-electro transfer device will be discussed. Similarly highlighted and discussed is the previously unknown potent one-pot preparation of carbon dots/polymer composites by thermal-induced carbonization of carbon source as carbon core and thermal-initiated in situ polymerization of monomers as surface passivation layer (i.e. matrix). This ability for one-pot preparation of carbon allotrope modified composites, as made evident by the paper, can and should be exploited for novel routes in the deliberate design of composites applied in many other high-demanding fields.

Citric acid (CA), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), o-dichlorobenzene, hexane and methanol were purchased from Kelong chemical industry (Chengdu). N-vinyl carbazole was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Tetra-n-butylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6), N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) and N,N-dimethyformamide (DMF) were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. Chloroform-d for NMR measurements was obtained from Beijing Ambic Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

The PVK-CDots were synthesized via thermal treating the mixture of citric acid and N-vinyl carbazole molecules. In typical procedures, 0.96 g of CA powder was pre-carbonized at about 200 ℃ for several minutes until the powder varied from white to light yellowish. Then a solid mixture of yellowish powder and N-vinyl carbazole (1.93 g) was dispersed into 10 ml of THF under ultrasonication for 5 min to form a transparent suspension. The obtained suspension was subsequently concentrated and placed into the vacuum drying chamber to remove the solvent THF. Thereafter, the dried solid was put into the furnace and treated at 300 ℃ for 2 h. After the process in furnace, the products were dispersed in 5 mL THF with vigorous sonication and then precipitated into 25 mL hot hexane and 25 mL hot methanol in sequence. Finally, the precipitates were dispersed in o-dichlorobenzene with sonication for 30 min and the resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove any insoluble or agglomerated larger particles. The obtained dark colored supernatant was concentrated into a small amount of liquid as PVK-CDots for further study.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of PVK-CDots was calculated using quinine sulfate (QYR = 0.55) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) as a reference, according to the following equation:
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y R
I
I R
A R
A
η 2
η R 2
Where A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and I is the integrated emission intensity calculated from the area under the emission peak on the same wavelength scale.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were taken from a JEOL JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 kV). The structure of CDots and PVK-CDots were characterized by an infrared spectrometer (IR, Bruker TENSOR 27). NMR measurements were carried out on a Bruker Advance 500 NMR spectrometer. The molecular weight of PVK-CDots was estimated by gel permeation chromatography (GPC, PL-GPC220). The U-3900 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) and LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America) were used to characterize the optical property of the PVK-CDots. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was conducted at room temperature in a tetra-n-butylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, 0.1 M) supported DMF solution using a three-electrode system consisting of a Pt foil as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and PVK-CDots deposited on Au disk as working electrode.

In general, carbon dots (CDots) are defined as small carbon nanoparticles possessing carboneous cores functionalized via diverse surface passivation schemes (Fig. 1 ). To synthesize CDots, two kinds of strategies have been reported which are deliberate chemical functionalization method and “one-pot” processing. The deliberate chemical functionalization method derived from the preliminary investigation of CDots is such that specifically processed carbon nanoparticles (around 5 nm in diameter) are chemically functionalized by oligomers or polymers for the purpose of surface passivation [21,22]. This method has been well known for the preparation of some high-performance CDots (with the fluorescence quantum yields above 50 % in the visible spectral range) [23]; the “one-pot” processing, however, is more popular in the literature, which is built on the carbonization of organic molecules or other naturally available carbon precursors [24–27]. Due to that the carbonization of organic precursors for constructing core carbon nanoparticles is incomplete, the residual organic species attached to or associated with the carbon nanoparticles would afford the required surface passivation function. For instance, when citric acid (CA)−oligomeric polyethyleneimine (PEI) mixtures have been used as precursors under the thermal carbonization condition, the CA is sacrificed for the formation of carboneous core while PEI, in large part, serves more of the function of surface passivation [28]. While a great many molecules and materials as precursors have been reported in the literature, the aim of CA as a carbon source has been extraordinarily prevailing in many publications [29–33].

To prepare PVK–CDots nanocomposites, our team has employed a deliberate chemical functionalization method. Briefly, commercially acquired carbon nano powders were processed in nitric acid to harvest smaller carbon nanoparticles. The nanoparticles were then taken part in the copolymerization reaction with N-vinylcarbazole (NVC) to obtain the PVK–CDots. Due to the fact that the carbon nanoparticles were introduced by copolymerization with monomer but not directly mixed with polymer, the carbon content of PVK-CDots (tested by TGA, 8 ∼ 10 %) was higher than that of most of reported carbon allotropes/polymer composites. However, the carbon content in PVK-CDots was still limited by the dispersibility of the carbon nanoparticles. It was because that in the synthesis, NVC and the small carbon nanoparticles had to be dispersed in a low amount of dimethylformamide (DMF) to form a homogenous mixture firstly.

In view of this, we designed a “one-pot” procedure to prepare PVK-CDots and to increase the carbon content by employing CA as a carbon source. During the carbonization of CA and the growth of carbon core, NVC was polymerized on the surface of the carbon cores by thermal initiation simultaneously, structural configuration adhering to the general definition on carbon dots (CDots) as small carbon nanoparticles with manifold surface passivation strategies. Structurally, the fluorescent carbon-based “quantum” dots (or often referred to as carbon dots) were small carbon nanoparticles with comparatively easy functionalization on particle surface [34]. At the same time, those carbon nanoparticles functionalized by PVK could seem as nanocomposites composed of CDots as fillers and PVK as matrix (noted as PVK-CDots).

Briefly, the CA was treated to a yellowish powder to dispersed well in THF, into which NVC was dispersed too. The mixture was kept stirring for overnight and then concentrated and placed into vacuum oven to remove the solvent. Thereafter, the dried solid mixture was treated at 300 °C for 2 h. After a series of washes, clear PVK-CDots were obtained. By using carbon source (CA) instead of carbon nanomaterials to mix with NVC, problems such as weak dispersibility of carbon materials and their uneven dispersion in the matrix were avoided, and the carbon content in the composites tested by TGA could be greatly increased to about 36 %.

The designed one-pot procedure proved to be successful by a “core-shell” structure of the PVK-CDots revealed by the TEM image (Fig. 2 ). The TEM observation indicated that discrete individual carbon cores were embedded in PVK polymer, and the average diameter of the core was about 5 nm. For comparison with the colorless morphology of PVK, the noticeably dark color must be correlated dominantly with the core in the PVK-CDots. The CA-derived carbon cores in PVK-CDots were amorphous in morphology with PVK surrounded. As shown in Figure S1, the XRD pattern of the PVK-CDots not only showed a broad peak of amorphous carbon core, but also showed sharp peaks belonging to the PVK at 18.7°, 19.5°, 20.1°, 23.6° and 28.3° [35].

The success of our preparation of PVK-CDots was also supported by the comparation of the fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopies of neat CDots (carbonized CA), N-vinylcarbazole and PVK-CDots. As shown in Figure S2, details were discussed in Supporting Information.

The polymeric nature of PVK-CDots prepared here and the control sample from NVC only without CA were confirmed in 1H NMR measurements and were compared with that of PVK-CDots synthesized by deliberate chemical functionalization method (PVK-CDots-C) and commercial PVK. In the experiments, 1H NMR spectra of the PVK-CDots in deuterated chloroform solution were acquired. High temperature to produce carbon core can lead to a radical polymerization reaction of NVC. As shown in Fig. 3 , 1H NMR of both PVK-CDots prepared in the paper and the control sample showed characteristic signals belonging to PVK, as the same as that of PVK-CDots-C we have reported and that of commercially acquired PVK.

Furthermore, we have estimated the average molecular weight of PVK polymers in the PVK-CDots based on the established end-group analysis method [36]. But the observed NMR signals were too broad, which indicated that the sample was beyond the limit of the method. In general, the limit of the method with the use of 1H NMR was for the polymers of a number-average molecular weight (Mn) not larger than 25,000 Da [37]. And according to the comparison with the 1H NMR spectrum of commercially acquired PVK of a known Mn value of 90,000 Da, a logical extrapolation was that the PVK polymers in the PVK-CDots were likely larger than 25,000 Da and maybe they were rather similar to that of commercially acquired PVK in terms of very broad signals.

To further ensure the polymeric nature of PVK-CDots, the average molecular weight of the PVK-CDots was estimated by GPC. Before measurement, the PVK-CDots in THF solution were filtrated through a 220 nm filter membrane to eliminate the influences from big particles. The molecular weight of PVK-CDots prepared from batch to batch sited between 5.5 × 105 ∼ 6.0 × 105.

As expected, on the one hand, the photon harvesting ability of PVK-CDots across visible spectral region was from the CA-produced carbon core. The molar absorption coefficient of PVK-CDots was 1.5 × 104 M−1 cm−1 which was comparative to that of the CA-produced CDots (1.1 × 104 M−1 cm−1). The CA-produced CDots were prepared through carbonizing the CA at 180 °C for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 4 , the PVK-CDots showed strong absorption in visible region, largely as the same as that of the PVK-CDots-C previously reported. However, in obvious contrast, the control sample exhibited only very weak or negligible visible absorption. Just like the inserted photos shown, both the o-dichlorobenzene solution of PVK-CDots (A) and the water solution of CA-produced CDots (D) were brown, but the o-dichlorobenzene solution of the control sample (C) was colorless. All above phenomenons gave a conclusion that the carboneous core played the vital role in determining the optical-harvesting ability, and the surface functionalization through varied organic species did not radically change visible-region absorption of the nanoparticles [38].

On the other hand, it was the PVK passivation layer that not only changed the aqueous-soluble carbon core to nonaqueous-soluble CDots but also increased the photoluminescence efficiency. The processability of the PVK-CDots in none-aqueous solvent was necessary for the built of solar cells [39,40]. The PVK-CDots sample was soluble in organic solvents such as THF, toluene and o-dichlorobenzene for colored solutions, but the CA-produced CDots was not (Fig. 4 inset A versus B). Similar to the PVK-CDots-C and other CDots obtained by deliberate chemical functionalization syntheses, such as the EDA-CDots that have been used as a benchmark [41], the PVK-CDots were brightly fluorescent in the green spectral range (Fig. 5 ), with calculated fluorescent quantum yields up to 40 % or higher at 400 nm, times higher than that of the CA-produced CDots, which was about 9.4 %. It was expected because surface passivation layer favored the surface-trapped electron/hole radiative recombination [42,43].

The excitation-dependent emission performance has been accepted as one of the characters to identify CDots [44,45]. As shown in Fig. 5, both the fluorescence emission wavelength and the quantum yield of the PVK-CDots were excitation wavelength dependent, which agreed well with other CDots [13]. As the excitation wavelength was increased, the emission band gradually shifted towards the red-wavelength region and the quantum yield tracked the progressive decline in absorption.

To our knowledge, CDots can also be characterized by their distinguished photoredox properties on the basis of fluorescence quenching effect upon the addition of both electron acceptor and donor quenchers, which made CDots much more attractive to diverse photon-electron conversion uses, including optoelectronic devices [17,18,46–48]. A common approach for the investigation of the redox properties has been the fluorescence quenching owing to charge-transfer process associated with electron rich or deficient molecules as quenchers in solution [17]. In the present paper 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) were chosen as excellent electron-accepting and electron-donating quenchers, respectively.

As displayed in Fig. 6 , a Stern-Volmer measurement was carried out for the fluorescence quenching of PVK-CDots provoked by DNT. Not merely a considerably high KSV value of 89 M−1 reflected by the linear relation portion of the plot, but also static quenching behavior appeared in the upward curvature from the linearity within relatively low quencher concentrations. In terms of average fluorescence lifetimes (τF) on the order of 5 ∼ 10 ns reported earlier in most CDots [49,50], the quenching rate constant (kq = KSV/τF) corresponding to the KSV value was higher than 8 × 109 M−1s−1, which exceeded the very upper-limit of diffusion control. Such a pronounced redox quenching efficiency could be attributable to a relatively rather large quenching radius beyond the dot radius, probably responsible for the static quenching process.

With N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) introduced as the efficient electron donor, the Stern-Volmer quenching constants (KSV) arising from the linear fitting of the plot for the PVK-CDots was evaluated 2.8 M−1. This value was lower than that of ∼18 M−1 reported previously for the redox quenching of the EDA-CDots as a benchmark, also one order of magnitude smaller than the KSV values founded with DNT as quencher suggesting that they were weak acceptors. However, the photoinduced redox performances of PVK-CDots reported here were similar to that of PVK-CDots-C (1.8 M−1) and N-ethylcarbazole functionalized CDots (NEC-CDots, 1.5 M−1) we had reported [18,34]. This phenomenon further proved that the carbazole moieties on the dot surface could obviously weaken the electron-accepting ability of CDots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application in Perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was conducted by employing a standard three-electrode system to determine the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of PVK-CDots. With the potential sweeping between -2.5 V and 2.5 V, as shown in Fig. 7 , the PVK-CDots exhibited apparently a pair of reduction-oxidation peaks. And the onset oxidation potential (EO OX) of the PVK-CDots was determined to be about 0.68 V. Then its HOMO energy level was calculated to be about -5.1 eV in accordance with the following equation [51];
(2)
H O M O = −
e
E O OX
+ 4 . 40
( e V )

Its LUMO level was calculated to be about -2.54 eV according to the following equation,
(3)
LUMO = HOMO +
E g
where Eg represented the band gap between HOMO and LUMO. The Eg of PVK-CDots was estimated to be 2.56 eV from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum by extrapolating the (ahv)2-hv curve (where a: absorbance, h: Plank’s constant, and v: frequency) [52], as can be seen in Figure S3, Supporting Information.

The PVK-CDots showed great potential in optoelectronic devices and systems not only owing to their non-aqueous-solubility but also benefiting from their photoinduced electron and hole propagation abilities. Especially, the PVK-CDots were qualified to act as a hole transport layer (HTL) in perovskite solar cells according to their HOMO energy level [53]. The HOMO energy level of PVK-CDots was slightly higher (0.3 eV) than the valence band energy of the perovskite (-5.4 eV), which led to a ladder-like band edge structure such that the holes from the HOMO level of the perovskite descended to the indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate through the PVK-CDots, thus facilitating efficient charge separation and hole propagation [54,55]. Apparently, such an appropriately narrow injection barrier between the perovskite and PVK-CDots enabled holes to be easily injected into the PVK-CDots for effective collection by the ITO electrode. In the other hand, it could be further speculated that the large band gap of PVK-CDots impeded transport of electrons from the the perovskite LUMO to the ITO electrode, also serving as an exceptional electron blocking layer material. The presence of PVK-CDots created an intermediate energy level that paved a good pathway for hole propagation and electron blocking, enabling the improved energy band alignment with the perovskite. The device architecture of perovskite solar cell was ITO/PVK-CDots/Perovskite/PC60BM/TiOX/Al and the corresponding energy band diagram was schematically proposed in Fig. 8 . This present work offers significant indicators for further research on exploring the properties of carbon nanoparticles as the highly efficient HTL material alternatives for enhancing the efficiency of perovskite solar cells. Moreover, the versatile surface functionalization of CDots alongside their environmentally friendliness, low-cost preparation, non-toxicity, and earth abundance, can open up a practical and cost-effective avenue to achieve highly efficient perovskite solar cells.

In conclusion, a new nanocomposite composed of poly (N-vinyl carbazole) functionalized carbon dots (PVK-CDots) is proposed and synthesized by a designed “one-pot” procedure that carbon core grown from citric acid under high temperature and is functionalized in situ by PVK. Taking the PVK-CDots as an example, we further make sure that CDots are promising candidates for developing solar cells when functionalized by polymeric photoconductive materials. The PVK-CDots are strong absorptive and therefore effective in photon harvesting and also brightly fluorescent across the visible spectral region as well as good solubility in common organic solvents. Photoluminescence quenching based on 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) serving as electron-accepting and electron-donating quenchers, respectively indicates that photo-induced charge transfer process occurs between PVK-CDots and quenchers, exhibiting great redox properties. More importantly, the synthesized PVK-CDots show that the HOMO energy level (HOMO = -5.1 eV) is close to that of perovskite, which is appropriate to ensure efficient hole transfer capability from the perovskite to PVK-CDots. Overall, we demonstrate here that PVK-CDots are potentially ideal candidates to act as the HTL in perovskite solar cells.

Yifeng Shen: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Chenghao Li: Software, Investigation. Yuping Wang: Methodology. Cailin Liu: Supervision. Xianyan Ren: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work.
We declare that there are no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Southwest University of Science and Technology of the Startup Foundation for Doctors [16ZX7139] and the Longshan Talent Program [17LZX675, 18LZXT01].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116326.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
